items.title: Cell-type-specific enrichment of somatic aneuploidy in the mammalian brain
items.author: Eran A. Mukamel, Hanqing Liu, M. Margarita Behrens, Joseph R. Ecker
items.content: <article>Mutations during brain development can alter the DNA of neurons, impacting their genetic identity. Using single-nucleus DNA methylation sequencing data from the adult mouse brain, Mukamel et al. identified chromosome losses and duplications (aneuploidies) in thousands of cells and associated them with brain regions and cell types.</article>

items.title: Novel approaches to clinical trial design in cancer neuroscience
items.author: Gregory Jones, Jennifer L. Anderson, Phuong T.T. Nguyen, Frederico O. Gleber-Netto, William L. Hwang, Vinay K. Puduvalli, Michelle Monje, Moran Amit
items.content: <article>Jones et al. survey new approaches in cancer neuroscience, including repurposing neuropsychiatric drugs and developing predictive biomarkers to guide innovative anti-cancer therapies and clinical trial designs.</article>

items.title: Entangled cellular and molecular relationships at the sensory neuron-cancer interface
items.author: Debpali Sur, Yi Zeng, Hiroki Kobayashi, Xiaofei Zhi, Mara R. Goetz, Clara M. Müller, Anastasia-Maria Zavitsanou, Caroline C. Picoli, Andre A. Martel Matos, Javier Pareja, Brajesh K. Savita, Taeho Lee, Jair P. Cunha-Junior, Jaime Henrique Amorim, Amin Reza Nikpoor, Alissa Dory, Ajitha Thanabalasuriar, Pedro A.F. Galante, Vincent T. Ma, Aaron W. James, Andrew J. Shepherd, Madeleine J. Oudin, Yuri L. Bunimovich, Nicole N. Scheff, Nisha J. D’Silva, Karen O. Dixon, Ishmail Abdus-Saboor, Timothy C. Wang, Sebastien Talbot, Alexander Birbrair
items.content: <article>Beyond acting as a passive route for nociceptive signals, sensory neurons are active participants in tumor progression. This review examines sensory neuron-tumor bidirectional interaction and explores its implications, with the ultimate goal of improving clinical outcomes across multiple cancer types.</article>

items.title: Cancer neuroscience of brain metastasis: When in Rome, do as the Romans do
items.author: Matthia A. Karreman, Frank Winkler
items.content: <article>In this comprehensive review, Karreman & Winkler explore how neural influences on the brain’s metastatic process govern brain tropism, colonization, and growth. Conversely, metastatic cancer has distinct effects on brain biology. These bidirectional neuro-cancer interactions can inform novel neuroscience-instructed cancer therapies.</article>

items.title: The neuroscience of brain cancers
items.author: Michelle Monje
items.content: <article>Neuron-cancer interactions are central to brain cancer pathophysiology. The perspective by Monje summarizes the current understanding of the neuroscience of brain cancers, poses key open questions, and highlights important future directions for the field of cancer neuroscience.</article>

items.title: Of hope from fading will: Interoceptive signaling and the behavioral biology of cachexia
items.author: Tobias Janowitz
items.content: <article>This NeuroView explores how systemic cancer signals induce behavioral changes via brain-body communication pathways, framing cachexia as an adaptive yet unsustainable interoceptive response. Recognizing patient-reported symptoms as biological signals offers new avenues for intervention and understanding brain-disease interactions.</article>

items.title: Wired to spread: Neural regulation of metastasis
items.author: Erica K. Sloan, Aeson Chang
items.content: <article>Neural signaling regulates multiple steps in the metastatic cascade. In this NeuroView, Sloan and Chang suggest that the neural landscape of metastatic microenvironments is highly dynamic and describe opportunities to target peripheral neuroplasticity for personalized anti-metastatic therapeutic interventions.</article>

items.title: Next stop: Cancer neuroscience
items.author: Benedicte Babayan, Ted Dobie
items.content: <article>Cancer neuroscience is an emerging and rapidly evolving interdisciplinary field that is reshaping our understanding of how the nervous system and cancers interact to affect underlying biology and clinical practice. We are pleased to present this special issue, which offers a snapshot of the field through a collection of perspectives, reviews, and NeuroViews that highlight the profound reciprocal influence between neural mechanisms and cancer—encompassing tumor initiation, progression, metastasis, and patient symptoms—as well as how cancer can reshape neural circuits and functions.</article>

items.title: Feeding-induced olfactory cortex suppression reduces satiation
items.author: Hung Lo, Walter Cañedo Riedel, Malinda L.S. Tantirigama, Anke Schoenherr, Laura Moreno Velasquez, Lukas Faiss, Amit Kumar, Aileen Hakus, Benjamin R. Rost, Matthew E. Larkum, Benjamin Judkewitz, Katharina Stumpenhorst, Marion Rivalan, York Winter, Eleonora Russo, Wolfgang Kelsch, Dietmar Schmitz, Friedrich W. Johenning
items.content: <article>Lo et al. show that binge feeding suppresses piriform (olfactory) cortex activity, reducing the brain’s representation of food flavor. This suppression is driven by the olfactory tubercle and promotes continued eating by decreasing sensory satiation. The findings link sensory suppression to overeating and highlight a cortical mechanism regulating appetite.</article>
